tagline: Calculate your estimated due date from last period
keywords: due date calculator, pregnancy due date, expected delivery date, pregnancy calculator
calcFn: var lmp = new Date(lmpYear, lmpMonth - 1, lmpDay); var due = new Date(lmp.getTime() + 280 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000); var today = new Date(); var diffMs = today - lmp; var weeks = Math.floor(diffMs / (7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000)); var days = Math.floor((diffMs % (7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000)) / (24 * 60 * 60 * 1000)); var tri = weeks < 13 ? 'First Trimester' : weeks < 27 ? 'Second Trimester' : weeks <= 42 ? 'Third Trimester' : 'Past due date'; var opts = {weekday:'long',month:'long',day:'numeric',year:'numeric'}; document.getElementById('dueDate').textContent = due.toLocaleDateString('en-US', opts); document.getElementById('weeksNow').textContent = weeks >= 0 && weeks <= 42 ? weeks + ' weeks, ' + days + ' days' : 'Check your dates'; document.getElementById('trimester').textContent = tri;
formulas: Naegele's Rule: Due Date = LMP + 280 days (40 weeks)
First Trimester: weeks 1-12, Second: 13-26, Third: 27-40
Only 5% of babies arrive on the exact due date

faq.question: How is the due date calculated?
faq.answer: The most common method (Naegele's Rule) adds 280 days (40 weeks) to the first day of your last menstrual period (LMP). This assumes a 28-day cycle with ovulation on day 14. Ultrasound dating may adjust this.

faq.question: How accurate is a due date?
faq.answer: Only about 5% of babies are born on their due date. Most arrive within 2 weeks before or after. Full-term is considered 39-40 weeks. Babies born 37-38 weeks are early term, 41+ weeks are late term.

faq.question: Can my due date change?
faq.answer: Yes. If an early ultrasound (before 13 weeks) differs from the LMP calculation by more than 5-7 days, your provider may adjust the due date. Ultrasound dating is most accurate in the first trimester.

tagline: Track recommended weight gain by trimester
keywords: pregnancy weight gain calculator, weight gain pregnancy, pregnancy weight tracker, bmi pregnancy
calcFn: var bmi = (preWeight / (height * height)) * 703; var cat = bmi < 18.5 ? 'under' : bmi < 25 ? 'normal' : bmi < 30 ? 'over' : 'obese'; var ranges = {under:[28,40,1.1], normal:[25,35,1.0], over:[15,25,0.6], obese:[11,20,0.5]}; var r = ranges[cat]; var midGain = (r[0] + r[1]) / 2; var weeklyRate2 = r[2]; var expectedNow = currentWeek <= 13 ? currentWeek * 0.2 : 2.5 + (currentWeek - 13) * weeklyRate2; document.getElementById('preBmi').textContent = fmt(bmi, 1) + ' (' + cat.replace('under','underweight').replace('normal','normal weight').replace('over','overweight') + ')'; document.getElementById('totalRec').textContent = r[0] + '-' + r[1] + ' lbs'; document.getElementById('gainToDate').textContent = fmt(Math.min(expectedNow, midGain), 1) + ' lbs (expected at week ' + currentWeek + ')'; document.getElementById('weeklyRate').textContent = fmt(weeklyRate2, 1) + ' lbs/week';
formulas: Underweight (BMI <18.5): gain 28-40 lbs
Normal (BMI 18.5-24.9): gain 25-35 lbs
Overweight (BMI 25-29.9): gain 15-25 lbs
Obese (BMI 30+): gain 11-20 lbs

faq.question: How much weight should I gain during pregnancy?
faq.answer: It depends on pre-pregnancy BMI. Normal weight: 25-35 lbs. Underweight: 28-40 lbs. Overweight: 15-25 lbs. Obese: 11-20 lbs. For twins, add 10-15 lbs to these ranges. Most gain happens in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters.

faq.question: Where does pregnancy weight go?
faq.answer: Baby: 7-8 lbs. Placenta: 1.5 lbs. Amniotic fluid: 2 lbs. Uterus: 2 lbs. Breast tissue: 2 lbs. Blood volume: 4 lbs. Fluids: 4 lbs. Fat stores: 5-9 lbs. Total: 27-33 lbs — it all has a purpose.

faq.question: Is it normal to lose weight in the first trimester?
faq.answer: Yes, mild weight loss (1-5 lbs) in the first trimester is common due to morning sickness and appetite changes. This usually doesn't affect the baby. Report weight loss over 5 lbs to your provider. Weight gain typically begins in the second trimester.

tagline: Check baby's weight and length vs growth charts
keywords: baby growth percentile, baby weight percentile, growth chart calculator, baby size chart
calcFn: var avgBoy = [7.5,9.6,11.5,13.0,14.3,15.4,16.4,17.3,18.1,18.8,19.4,20.0,20.5,21.0,21.5,22.0,22.5,23.0,23.4,23.8,24.3,24.7,25.1,25.5,25.9]; var avgGirl = [7.1,9.0,10.8,12.2,13.4,14.5,15.4,16.2,17.0,17.7,18.3,18.8,19.3,19.8,20.3,20.7,21.1,21.5,22.0,22.4,22.8,23.2,23.6,24.0,24.4]; var avgs = gender === 'boy' ? avgBoy : avgGirl; var idx = Math.min(Math.round(ageMonths), 24); var avgW = avgs[idx]; var zScore = (weightLbs - avgW) / (avgW * 0.12); var pctile = Math.min(99, Math.max(1, Math.round(50 + zScore * 20))); var cat = pctile < 5 ? 'Below 5th — discuss with pediatrician' : pctile < 25 ? 'Lower range — likely normal' : pctile < 75 ? 'Average range' : pctile < 95 ? 'Upper range — likely normal' : 'Above 95th — discuss with pediatrician'; document.getElementById('percentile').textContent = pctile + 'th percentile'; document.getElementById('category').textContent = cat; document.getElementById('avg').textContent = fmt(avgW, 1) + ' lbs (50th percentile)';
formulas: Based on WHO/CDC growth chart data
Percentile shows what % of babies weigh less than yours
50th percentile = average; 5th-95th is normal range
Consistent growth along any percentile curve is healthy

faq.question: What is a normal baby weight percentile?
faq.answer: Any percentile between 5th and 95th is considered normal. What matters most is consistent growth along the same curve over time, not the specific percentile. A baby consistently at the 20th percentile is healthy.

faq.question: Should I worry about a low percentile?
faq.answer: Not necessarily. Genetics play a big role — small parents tend to have small babies. Concerns arise when a baby drops more than 2 percentile lines rapidly, or falls below the 3rd percentile. Talk to your pediatrician about any concerns.

faq.question: How often should I weigh my baby?
faq.answer: At regular checkups: 2 weeks, 1, 2, 4, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 24 months. Avoid daily weighing — normal fluctuations cause unnecessary worry. Weekly weights are useful for breastfed newborns in the first month if there are feeding concerns.

tagline: Check safe breastmilk storage times by temperature
keywords: breastmilk storage calculator, breast milk storage times, milk storage guide, pumped milk storage
calcFn: var limits = {counter:{max:4,unit:'hours',best:'Use within 2 hours'},cooler:{max:24,unit:'hours',best:'Transfer to fridge ASAP'},fridge:{max:4,unit:'days',best:'Use within 3 days for best quality'},freezer:{max:180,unit:'days',best:'Use within 3 months for best quality'},deepfreeze:{max:365,unit:'days',best:'Use within 6 months for best quality'}}; var info = limits[storageType]; var expired = false; if (info.unit === 'hours') expired = pumpDate * 24 > info.max; else expired = pumpDate > info.max; document.getElementById('maxTime').textContent = info.max + ' ' + info.unit; document.getElementById('status').textContent = expired ? 'EXPIRED — discard this milk' : 'Safe to use (' + pumpDate + ' days stored)'; document.getElementById('bestPractice').textContent = info.best;
formulas: Room temperature: up to 4 hours
Insulated cooler: up to 24 hours
Refrigerator (40°F): up to 4 days
Freezer (0°F): up to 6 months
Deep freezer (-4°F): up to 12 months

faq.question: How long can breastmilk sit out?
faq.answer: Freshly expressed breastmilk can sit at room temperature (77°F/25°C or below) for up to 4 hours. In a hot room, use within 1-2 hours. Once baby has started feeding from a bottle, use within 2 hours.

faq.question: Can I mix fresh and frozen breastmilk?
faq.answer: Yes, but cool the fresh milk in the fridge first before adding to frozen. Never add warm milk directly to frozen. Label with the date of the oldest milk. The storage clock starts from the oldest milk's date.

faq.question: How do I thaw frozen breastmilk?
faq.answer: Best: overnight in the fridge. Quick: run under warm (not hot) water or use a bottle warmer. Never microwave — it creates hot spots and destroys nutrients. Once thawed, use within 24 hours. Never refreeze.

tagline: Calculate formula amount by age and weight
keywords: baby formula calculator, formula feeding amount, how much formula, infant feeding calculator
calcFn: var ozDay = Math.min(weightLbs * 2.5, 32); if (ageMonths >= 6) ozDay = Math.min(ozDay, 28); var feeds = ageMonths < 1 ? 10 : ageMonths < 3 ? 8 : ageMonths < 6 ? 6 : 5; var perFeed = ozDay / feeds; var scoopsDay = ozDay / 2; var gramsDay = scoopsDay * 8.7; var tinsMonth = (gramsDay * 30) / 354; document.getElementById('ozPerDay').textContent = fmt(ozDay, 0) + ' oz'; document.getElementById('ozPerFeed').textContent = fmt(perFeed, 1) + ' oz every ' + fmt(24/feeds, 1) + ' hours'; document.getElementById('feedsPerDay').textContent = feeds + ' times/day'; document.getElementById('monthlyTins').textContent = fmt(tinsMonth, 1) + ' cans (~' + dollar(tinsMonth * 20) + '/month)';
formulas: General rule: 2.5 oz per pound of body weight per day
Maximum: 32 oz/day (do not exceed without medical advice)
Newborn feeds: 8-10x/day, 3mo: 6-8x/day, 6mo+: 5-6x/day

faq.question: How much formula does a baby need?
faq.answer: General guideline: 2.5 ounces per pound of body weight per day, up to 32 oz max. A 10-lb baby needs about 25 oz/day. After 6 months when solids begin, formula intake may decrease to 24-28 oz/day.

faq.question: How do I know if my baby is eating enough?
faq.answer: Signs of adequate feeding: 6+ wet diapers/day, steady weight gain (4-7 oz/week in first months), content between feedings, meeting developmental milestones. Report any concerns to your pediatrician.

faq.question: How much does formula cost per month?
faq.answer: Brand name: $150-250/month. Store brand: $100-150/month. Specialty (hypoallergenic): $200-400/month. A baby uses about 3-4 cans of powdered formula per month. Generic brands are nutritionally equivalent to name brands.

tagline: Track contraction frequency and duration for labor
keywords: contraction timer, contraction calculator, labor timer, contraction tracker
calcFn: var perHr = 60 / frequency; var stage = ''; var action = ''; if (duration >= 60 && frequency <= 4 && consistent === 'yes') { stage = 'Active Labor (4-1-1 met)'; action = 'Go to the hospital NOW'; } else if (duration >= 45 && frequency <= 5) { stage = 'Active Labor (approaching)'; action = 'Call your provider and prepare to go'; } else if (duration >= 30 && frequency <= 8) { stage = 'Early Labor'; action = 'Rest, hydrate, time contractions. Call if water breaks.'; } else { stage = 'Pre-labor / Braxton Hicks'; action = 'Rest and monitor. These may not be true labor contractions.'; } document.getElementById('stage').textContent = stage; document.getElementById('action').textContent = action; document.getElementById('perHour').textContent = fmt(perHr, 1) + ' per hour';
formulas: 4-1-1 Rule: contractions 4 min apart, lasting 1 min, for 1 hour = go to hospital
Early labor: contractions 5-20 min apart, 30-45 seconds
Active labor: contractions 3-5 min apart, 45-60+ seconds

faq.question: When should I go to the hospital?
faq.answer: The 4-1-1 rule: when contractions are 4 minutes apart, lasting 1 minute each, for at least 1 hour. Also go immediately if: water breaks, heavy bleeding, baby stops moving, or you feel something is wrong.

faq.question: How do I time contractions?
faq.answer: Time from the START of one contraction to the START of the next (that's the frequency). Also time how long each contraction lasts (duration). Record at least 4-5 in a row to establish a pattern.

faq.question: What is the difference between Braxton Hicks and real contractions?
faq.answer: Braxton Hicks: irregular, don't get stronger, stop with position change or hydration. Real labor: regular pattern, progressively stronger and closer together, don't stop with rest, may radiate to back. When in doubt, call your provider.

tagline: Find the right diaper size from baby's weight
keywords: diaper size calculator, diaper size chart, baby diaper size, diaper weight chart
calcFn: var size, range, daily; if (babyWeight <= 6) { size = 'Preemie'; range = 'Up to 6 lbs'; daily = 12; } else if (babyWeight <= 10) { size = 'Newborn (N)'; range = '6-10 lbs'; daily = 10; } else if (babyWeight <= 14) { size = 'Size 1'; range = '8-14 lbs'; daily = 10; } else if (babyWeight <= 18) { size = 'Size 2'; range = '12-18 lbs'; daily = 9; } else if (babyWeight <= 28) { size = 'Size 3'; range = '16-28 lbs'; daily = 8; } else if (babyWeight <= 37) { size = 'Size 4'; range = '22-37 lbs'; daily = 7; } else if (babyWeight <= 50) { size = 'Size 5'; range = '27-50 lbs'; daily = 6; } else { size = 'Size 6'; range = '35+ lbs'; daily = 5; } var monthlyCost = daily * 30 * 0.25; document.getElementById('diaperSize').textContent = size; document.getElementById('weightRange').textContent = range; document.getElementById('dailyCount').textContent = daily + ' diapers/day'; document.getElementById('monthlyCost').textContent = dollar(monthlyCost) + '/month (~' + dollar(0.25) + '/diaper)';
formulas: Diaper sizes overlap in weight ranges for a comfortable fit
Newborns use ~10-12 diapers/day, toddlers ~5-7/day
Average cost: $0.20-0.35 per diaper depending on brand

faq.question: How do I know when to size up?
faq.answer: Signs you need the next size: frequent leaks or blowouts, red marks on thighs or waist, difficulty fastening tabs, the diaper looks too small. It's better to size up early than deal with leaks.

faq.question: How many diapers does a baby use?
faq.answer: Newborn: 10-12/day. 3 months: 8-10/day. 6 months: 7-8/day. 12 months: 6-7/day. Toddler: 5-6/day. That's roughly 2,500-3,000 diapers in the first year, costing $600-900.

faq.question: Are store brand diapers as good as name brand?
faq.answer: Many parents find store brands (Up&Up, Parent's Choice, Kirkland) work just as well at 40-50% less cost. Premium brands may fit better for some body types. Try a small pack before committing to a large box.

tagline: Calculate your Holmes-Rahe life stress score
keywords: stress score calculator, holmes rahe stress scale, life stress test, stress level calculator
calcFn: var score = majorLife * 40 + workStress * 8 + finStress * 8 + healthStress * 6 + relStress * 6; var risk = score >= 300 ? 'High Risk — 80% chance of health impact' : score >= 200 ? 'Moderate Risk — 50% chance of health impact' : score >= 150 ? 'Mild Risk — 30% chance of health impact' : 'Low Risk'; var rec = score >= 200 ? 'Consider professional support. Prioritize stress management, sleep, and social connection.' : score >= 150 ? 'Practice daily stress management: exercise, meditation, adequate sleep.' : 'Your stress level is manageable. Maintain healthy habits.'; document.getElementById('totalScore').textContent = fmt(score, 0) + ' points'; document.getElementById('riskLevel').textContent = risk; document.getElementById('recommendation').textContent = rec;
formulas: Score based on Holmes-Rahe stress inventory principles
Major life events weighted heavily (40 pts each)
Below 150: low risk, 150-200: mild, 200-300: moderate, 300+: high

faq.question: What is the Holmes-Rahe Stress Scale?
faq.answer: Developed in 1967 by psychiatrists Holmes and Rahe, this scale assigns point values to 43 life events. Higher scores correlate with higher risk of stress-related illness. Scores above 300 indicate an 80% risk of health impact within 2 years.

faq.question: What counts as a major life event?
faq.answer: Top stressors: death of spouse (100), divorce (73), separation (65), jail (63), death of close family (63), injury/illness (53), job loss (47), retirement (45), pregnancy (40), moving (20). Even positive events like marriage (50) add stress.

faq.question: Can stress actually make you sick?
faq.answer: Yes. Chronic stress elevates cortisol, weakens the immune system, raises blood pressure, and increases inflammation. It's linked to heart disease, depression, digestive issues, insomnia, and weakened immunity. Stress management is a health priority, not a luxury.

tagline: Score your sleep quality with PSQI-inspired assessment
keywords: sleep quality calculator, sleep score, psqi sleep assessment, sleep quality test
calcFn: var durationScore = hoursSlept >= 7 ? 3 : hoursSlept >= 6 ? 2 : hoursSlept >= 5 ? 1 : 0; var latencyScore = minsToFall <= 15 ? 3 : minsToFall <= 30 ? 2 : minsToFall <= 60 ? 1 : 0; var wakeScore = wakeUps === 0 ? 3 : wakeUps <= 1 ? 2 : wakeUps <= 3 ? 1 : 0; var restScore = restedness >= 8 ? 3 : restedness >= 6 ? 2 : restedness >= 4 ? 1 : 0; var total = durationScore + latencyScore + wakeScore + restScore; var maxPossible = 12; var pctScore = (total / maxPossible) * 100; var rating = pctScore >= 80 ? 'Excellent' : pctScore >= 60 ? 'Good' : pctScore >= 40 ? 'Fair' : 'Poor'; var timeInBed = hoursSlept + minsToFall / 60 + wakeUps * 0.17; var efficiency = (hoursSlept / timeInBed) * 100; var tip = hoursSlept < 7 ? 'Increase sleep time — aim for 7-9 hours.' : minsToFall > 30 ? 'Improve sleep onset: limit screens 1hr before bed, keep room cool.' : wakeUps > 2 ? 'Reduce wake-ups: avoid caffeine after 2pm, limit alcohol.' : 'Your sleep habits are solid. Maintain consistency.'; document.getElementById('sleepScore').textContent = total + '/' + maxPossible + ' (' + fmt(pctScore, 0) + '%)'; document.getElementById('rating').textContent = rating; document.getElementById('efficiency').textContent = pct(Math.min(100, efficiency)); document.getElementById('tips').textContent = tip;
formulas: Score based on PSQI components: duration, latency, wake-ups, restedness
Each component scored 0-3 (3 = best)
Sleep efficiency = Time asleep ÷ Time in bed × 100
Good efficiency: 85%+, Excellent: 90%+

faq.question: How much sleep do adults need?
faq.answer: Adults need 7-9 hours per night (National Sleep Foundation). Older adults (65+): 7-8 hours. Consistently sleeping less than 6 hours is associated with increased risk of obesity, diabetes, heart disease, and cognitive decline.

faq.question: What is sleep efficiency?
faq.answer: Sleep efficiency = time actually sleeping ÷ time in bed × 100. Above 85% is good, above 90% is excellent. If you're in bed 8 hours but only sleeping 6, your efficiency is 75% — and you should address the underlying issues.

faq.question: How can I fall asleep faster?
faq.answer: Key strategies: consistent bed/wake times, no screens 60 min before bed, cool room (65-68°F), no caffeine after 2pm, relaxation routine (reading, meditation), avoid alcohol close to bedtime (it disrupts sleep quality).

tagline: Screen anxiety levels with GAD-7 inspired assessment
keywords: anxiety score calculator, gad 7 anxiety test, anxiety screening, anxiety level test
calcFn: var total = nervous + worry + restless + irritable + afraid; var sev = total <= 4 ? 'Minimal Anxiety' : total <= 9 ? 'Mild Anxiety' : total <= 14 ? 'Moderate Anxiety' : 'Severe Anxiety'; var rec = total >= 10 ? 'Consider speaking with a mental health professional. This score suggests clinical-level anxiety.' : total >= 5 ? 'Mild anxiety is common. Try mindfulness, exercise, and stress management. Seek help if symptoms worsen.' : 'Your anxiety level appears minimal. Continue healthy coping strategies.'; document.getElementById('totalScore').textContent = total + ' / 15'; document.getElementById('severity').textContent = sev; document.getElementById('recommendation').textContent = rec;
formulas: Based on GAD-7 screening principles (simplified 5-item version)
Rate each item: 0=not at all, 1=several days, 2=more than half, 3=nearly every day
0-4: Minimal, 5-9: Mild, 10-14: Moderate, 15+: Severe

faq.question: What is the GAD-7?
faq.answer: The GAD-7 (Generalized Anxiety Disorder 7) is a validated screening tool used by healthcare providers worldwide. It asks about anxiety symptoms over the past 2 weeks. A score of 10+ suggests clinical anxiety warranting professional evaluation.

faq.question: Is anxiety normal?
faq.answer: Some anxiety is normal and even helpful (motivating preparation, alerting to danger). Anxiety becomes a concern when it's excessive, hard to control, interferes with daily life, or causes physical symptoms (rapid heart rate, trouble breathing, insomnia).

faq.question: When should I seek help for anxiety?
faq.answer: Seek professional help if: anxiety interferes with work, relationships, or daily activities; you avoid situations due to fear; you have panic attacks; physical symptoms persist (insomnia, GI issues, muscle tension); or you're using substances to cope.

tagline: Screen for depression with PHQ-9 inspired assessment

Seek help if worsening.' : 'Your score suggests minimal depression. Maintain healthy routines and social connections.'; document.getElementById('totalScore').textContent = total + ' / 15'; document.getElementById('severity').textContent = sev; document.getElementById('recommendation').textContent = rec;
formulas: Based on PHQ-9 screening principles (simplified 5-item version)
Rate each item: 0=not at all, 1=several days, 2=more than half, 3=nearly every day
0-4: Minimal, 5-9: Mild, 10-14: Moderate, 15+: Severe

faq.question: What is the PHQ-9?
faq.answer: The PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) is the most widely used depression screening tool. It asks about 9 symptoms over the past 2 weeks. A score of 10+ suggests major depressive disorder and warrants professional evaluation.

faq.question: Is this a diagnosis?
faq.answer: No. This is a screening tool, not a diagnostic instrument. Only a qualified healthcare provider can diagnose depression. If your score suggests moderate or severe depression, please consult a mental health professional.

faq.question: What help is available?
faq.answer: 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (call or text 988). Crisis Text Line (text HOME to 741741). Therapy (CBT is very effective for depression). Medication (SSRIs are first-line treatment). Exercise, social connection, and sleep hygiene also help significantly.

tagline: Score your work-life balance across key areas
keywords: work life balance calculator, work life balance score, burnout risk, work stress assessment
calcFn: var workScore = workHours <= 40 ? 25 : workHours <= 45 ? 20 : workHours <= 50 ? 15 : workHours <= 55 ? 10 : 5; var exScore = exercise >= 4 ? 25 : exercise >= 3 ? 20 : exercise >= 2 ? 15 : exercise >= 1 ? 10 : 5; var socScore = socialHours >= 10 ? 20 : socialHours >= 5 ? 15 : socialHours >= 2 ? 10 : 5; var slpScore = sleepHours >= 7.5 ? 20 : sleepHours >= 7 ? 15 : sleepHours >= 6 ? 10 : 5; var vacScore = vacation >= 15 ? 10 : vacation >= 10 ? 8 : vacation >= 5 ? 5 : 2; var total = workScore + exScore + socScore + slpScore + vacScore; var grade = total >= 80 ? 'A — Excellent Balance' : total >= 65 ? 'B — Good Balance' : total >= 50 ? 'C — Needs Attention' : total >= 35 ? 'D — At Risk' : 'F — Burnout Risk'; var issues = [{name:'Reduce work hours',score:workScore},{name:'Exercise more',score:exScore},{name:'More social/hobby time',score:socScore},{name:'Improve sleep',score:slpScore},{name:'Take more vacation',score:vacScore}]; issues.sort(function(a,b){return a.score - b.score}); document.getElementById('balanceScore').textContent = total + ' / 100'; document.getElementById('grade').textContent = grade; document.getElementById('topIssue').textContent = issues[0].name;
formulas: Work hours: 40hr=25pts, 50hr=15pts, 60hr+=5pts
Exercise: 4+/week=25pts, 2/week=15pts, 0=5pts
Social/hobbies: 10+hr=20pts, 5hr=15pts
Sleep: 7.5+hr=20pts, 6hr=10pts
Vacation: 15+days=10pts, 5days=5pts

faq.question: What is good work-life balance?
faq.answer: There's no universal standard, but key indicators include: working 40-45 hours/week, exercising 3+ times/week, getting 7+ hours sleep, having meaningful social connections, and using your vacation time. The right balance is personal.

faq.question: How many hours is too many to work?
faq.answer: Research shows productivity drops significantly after 50 hours/week and collapses after 55. The WHO found working 55+ hours/week increases stroke risk by 35% and heart disease by 17%. Sustainable high performance requires recovery.

faq.question: How can I improve work-life balance?
faq.answer: Set firm boundaries (no email after 7pm), schedule personal time like meetings, take your vacation days, learn to say no, batch similar tasks, delegate, and remember that being busy isn't the same as being productive.

tagline: Get recommended daily meditation based on your goals
keywords: meditation time calculator, how long to meditate, meditation duration, mindfulness timer
calcFn: var times = {beginner:{stress:5,focus:5,sleep:10,anxiety:5,spiritual:10},some:{stress:10,focus:15,sleep:15,anxiety:10,spiritual:15},regular:{stress:15,focus:20,sleep:15,anxiety:15,spiritual:25},advanced:{stress:20,focus:30,sleep:20,anxiety:20,spiritual:45}}; var mins = ((times[experience]||times.beginner)[goal])||10; document.getElementById('recTime').textContent = mins + ' minutes/day'; document.getElementById('weeklyTotal').textContent = (mins * 7) + ' minutes/week'; var notes = {stress:'8 weeks of daily meditation reduces cortisol by 23% (JAMA study).',focus:'Just 10 min/day improves attention span within 2 weeks (University of Waterloo).',sleep:'Mindfulness meditation improves sleep quality comparable to sleep medication (JAMA Internal Medicine).',anxiety:'MBSR programs (8 weeks) reduce anxiety as effectively as medication for some people.',spiritual:'Experienced meditators show measurable brain changes in compassion and empathy regions.'}; document.getElementById('scienceNote').textContent = notes[goal] || '';
formulas: Beginners: start with 5 minutes, build by 1-2 min/week
Most benefits seen at 10-20 minutes daily
Consistency matters more than duration
Research shows benefits start with as little as 5 min/day

faq.question: How long should I meditate?
faq.answer: Start with 5 minutes if you're new. Research shows meaningful benefits begin at 10-15 minutes daily. Advanced practitioners often sit for 20-45 minutes. The key is consistency — 5 minutes daily beats 30 minutes occasionally.

faq.question: What type of meditation should I try?
faq.answer: For stress: body scan or progressive relaxation. For focus: breath counting or concentration meditation. For sleep: yoga nidra or guided relaxation. For anxiety: mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR). Try several and see what resonates.

faq.question: How long until I see benefits?
faq.answer: Many people notice reduced stress within 1-2 weeks of daily practice. Sleep improvements often occur within 2-4 weeks. Brain structure changes (gray matter density) are measurable after 8 weeks of regular practice (Harvard/MGH study).
